title: Chapter 39
text: Epilogue R onin walked along the sidewalk, optics roaming over the expanse of open land across the street. As many times as he’d seen the park in the forty-six years since coming to Cheyenne, he never tired of it — the place was ever-changing. For the last few decades, most of it had been repurposed for farming. Coupled with the fields outside of town, Cheyenne had a food surplus that attracted traders from miles around . Children ran and played between rows of crops, laughing and smiling, especially when fieldworkers shooed them away. Ronin knew them all by name, knew which of them were likely to leave signs on Mr. Mather’s back. The teacher still hadn’t caught on to their tricks . “ Dad !” Stopping, Ronin turned. Tabitha jogged toward him, a bundle of red flowers in her hand. Ronin’s memory flashed back to the first time he’d held his daughter. Tabitha had been so tiny and fragile, small enough to hold in one hand. It hadn’t mattered that her biological father was someone else; Ronin bonded with her instantly . Now, Tabitha was a grown woman with children of her own. She possessed so many of Lara’s features — the red hair, the bright blue eyes, the stubborn streak. The thirty-six years since her birth had gone by quickly, though they’d been full of joy and meaning . Tabitha grinned as she reached him. “Found these on my way back from the fields. I thought mom would like them .” Ronin smiled, too, and accepted the flowers. “She’ll love them .” “How’s she been? I visited with the kids yesterday, but I think they tired her out pretty quick .” Ronin cupped Tabitha’s cheek with his free hand, brushing a thumb over her light dusting of freckles. “She tires easily. Not that those kids don’t tire me out , too .” She chuckled. “Tell me about it. I gotta run — Dan’s waiting for me. We’re going to do some repairs on the wall. Let mom know I’ll stop by tomorrow. The kids have school and it’s my day off, so I can help her around the house if she needs it .” “I’ll let her know .” Tabitha stepped forward and wrapped her arms around him, kissing his cheek. “Love you guys .” He hugged her back and kissed her hair, as he had throughout her youth. “Love you too, Tabby .” Ronin watched her go. Had he made the right decision? Tabitha wasn’t a child anymore. She knew Lara’s health was waning, knew that, despite all the comforts, death was a reality in Cheyenne. She’d been old enough to understand what was happening during the famine, and was nearly twenty when sickness swept through the town and killed dozens . But he couldn’t’ bring himself to say anything that would kill the joy always sparkling in her eyes . Finally, he resumed his walk, greeting the neighbors who were tending their gardens. Flowers had become popular in recent years, but most folk still grew their own crops — the struggles of the past were not forgotten here, though so many of the people who had suffered under Warlord and risen up against him were gone. Their legacies continued, carried by their children and grandchildren, new generations who worked alongside bots to build a prosperous community . He opened the front gate and entered the yard, closing it gently behind him. Sometimes it was still strange, coming home like this, especially with his memories of the time before. Once, this had just been a place to store and care for his gear. A temporary stop between long journeys into the Dust. A building devoid of life and largely empty of purpose . But Lara had made it a home . Ronin climbed the steps onto the porch. Chimes — crafted from scrap metal Lara had collected over the years — tinkled quietly in the breeze. The same sound that had drawn him to her, nearly half a century ago. The lure of fate . He stepped inside and called her name as he shut the door behind him. The living room didn’t have a single patch of bare wall; Lara had remained a collector, as she dubbed herself, throughout her life. Decades-worth of trinkets adorned the walls and numerous shelves, displayed proudly for any guests . “In here,” she said . Ronin followed her voice toward the kitchen, passing the seashells she’d gathered when they visited the western ocean. There was a jar of sand from the same trip on one of the end tables, a heart-shaped rock pressed against the glass. Ronin hadn’t understood the need to bring more sand back to a town already full of dust and dirt, but he had carried it nonetheless . Lara stood at the counter, smearing jam onto a slice of bread. Her eyes were fixed on the task, her movements slow and deliberate, but she looked up at him the moment he stepped into the room. She smiled, wrinkles bunching at the corners of her eyes . Her hair was gray, now; she’d panicked when she found her first gray hair, locking herself in the bathroom for more than an hour before he finally forced the door open to ensure she was okay. She’d taken it as a sign of her mortality, convinced he’d leave her at any moment . But she was as beautiful to him now as when he first saw her. Even more so, after the life they’d shared . “Tabby found some flowers for you,” he said, holding up the bundle . “She could’ve brought them herself,” she said. Ronin didn’t miss the trembling in her hand as she set the knife down. “She only lives three houses away, and I would have loved to see her .” “She and Dan are helping with repairs on the wall, which means she was already running late. Tomorrow is her free day. She said she’ll come by .” Lara stared down at the jellied bread, smiling. “I’m so proud of her. Of all of them .” “They had the best mother this side of the Dust,” Ronin replied, smiling as well. Though Tabitha had been Lara’s only natural-born child — complications during the birth had made it too risky to try again — they’d adopted and raised three other children, all loved just as much as Tabby. Samuel, Melissa, and Amanda . Lara chuckled and lifted her arms toward him. “Are you just going to stand there, or are you going to come say hello ?” Ronin crossed the room and embraced her, gently but warmly, running a hand along her silver braid . “Hello,” he said, and dipped his head to kiss her . “Hello,” she returned. “Would you put them in water ?” “Of course.” He placed the flowers on the counter, picked up her bread, and took her arm. She leaned on him as she walked to the table, where he pulled a chair out with his boot and helped her ease into it . “You’re always so good to me .” “It’s no less than you deserve, Lara Brooks .” “Still calling me Brooks after all this time.” She shook her head, but there was mirth in her eyes as she picked up the bread and took a bite . Ronin found a vase for the flowers and filled it with water from the sink. “I’ve told you already, just because I married you without having a surname of my own doesn’t mean you get to drop yours.” Returning to the table, he placed the vase at its center . She’d only taken two bites when she set the bread down and raised a hand to her forehead. “It’s too early to be so tired .” “Sunset is in less than an hour,” Ronin said. “And when have you ever passed up an opportunity for me to take you to bed, anyway ?” “We can go upstairs, as long as you promise not to rip my shirt this time,” she replied, but her smile was strained, her face too pale . “ Come , then .” He pulled out her chair and lifted her into his arms. Lara’s weight had always been slight to him, but she was thinner now than ever before. She slid her arms around his neck and rested her head on his shoulder as he carried her up to the bedroom . Ronin set her on her feet beside the bed and she took his face between her hands, kissing him. He returned the kiss and undressed her. His hands brushed lightly over her skin — so changed, and yet so familiar — lingering on the stretch marks, the scars. It was evidence of a hard-fought life, made all the more precious by its struggles . “You remain the most beautiful thing I have ever seen,” he said . “You’ve always made me feel that way .” He helped her into her nightgown and pulled back the covers. She slipped into bed slowly. Ronin kicked off his boots and changed out of his dirty clothes, putting on a plain shirt and a pair of shorts. He climbed onto the bed and drew her into his arms. Her body still fit against his perfectly . Lara’s light breath tickled the sensors on his skin. He took her braid in one hand, absently running his fingertips over its length . “I never thought I’d have this life,” she said softly . “Neither did I. It is more than I could have ever hoped .” She reached up and brushed her fingers over his jaw. “Even after all these years, I haven’t had enough time to love you .” Her words pierced him; they were too final, too true. He settled a hand on her cheek and ran his thumbs over the fine wrinkles near her eye. Ten thousand years wouldn’t be enough, but this…it was still too soon . “We’ll have years more, still,” he said . “Bots always say what they mean,” Lara said, “but that doesn’t make it the truth .” “ Lara , I — ” “Shh.” She placed a finger over his lips. Her smile was warm, though her eyes were tired. “Will you stay with me while I sleep ?” “You know I always do,” he replied . “And you know I always ask .” She relaxed, her weight settling against him, and soon her breathing evened out. He measured time by the slow, faint beat of her heart, pulling up memories from their lives together . Her sunburn after her first day working in the fields, despite covering herself. The joyous smile she’d worn the first time she held Tabitha and the tears that followed. Her patience with the townsfolk when they came to her — the mother of the revolution that set them free — for advice. And the life glowing in her eyes when she danced, just for him . Two hours and eleven minutes after midnight, she released her final breath. It brushed over Ronin’s skin gently, the last caress from the woman he loved with all the power in his processors. He held her close and shut his eyes, pressing his lips to her hair . “I will love you even after darkness takes me,” he said, softly . * * * “M om? Dad?” Tabitha called as she stepped through the front door of her parents’ home. Silence greeted her. She checked the kitchen. A plate with a half-eaten slice of jellied bread was upon the table, as well as the flowers she’d picked . “Mom?” she called again, unable to keep the worry from her voice. It was early, and her mother had been more tired than usual lately…though shouldn’t her father have answered ? She hurried upstairs. The door to their bedroom was ajar, and her heart pounded as she approached it. “You guys in here?” she asked, pushing the door open all the way . Somehow, she knew the moment she looked at them. She approached the bed, covering her mouth with a hand as tears blurred her vision. Her mother lay with her father, both of them still. The casing on his back was pried open, the power cell in his hand . Blinking away her tears, she picked up the folded paper that lay atop him with a trembling hand. She wiped her eyes and read it . T abby, Sam, Melissa, and Amanda , I am so sorry to make this your burden. You may not understand, not yet, but I hope you will, in time. We have lived long, happy lives together. My place remains at your mother’s side. I cannot continue on, unchanging, as my children and grandchildren live and die . I ask that you do not reactivate me. Please bury me with your mother, so I can fulfill my promise and be with her during her long sleep . Know that we are proud of all of you and the people you have grown to be, and that our love will always be with you. When your mother was young, we changed the world. It is up to you to continue to shape it into something beautiful for everyone . With Love , Dad
